question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an unrecognized mode string via os.Args[1] to trigger os.Exit(2) repeatedly through an orchestration loop, causing the container to restart and re-establish gRPC connections to all network processing nodes on every restart, increasing their connection-handling load by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any rate-limiting or argument sanitization on os.Args[1] to invoke the healthcheck binary in rapid succession with the 'live' mode, causing repeated os.Stat('/tmp/alive') filesystem calls that starve the heartbeat goroutine of I/O bandwidth and indirectly stall transaction submission to network nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the liveness probe to return exit code 1 by making /tmp/alive appear stale (age > 2*time.Minute), triggering a Kubernetes pod restart that forces the pinger to re-initialize all gRPC connections to every network processing node, amplifying connection-setup CPU load on those nodes by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 2-minute staleness threshold in the 'live' branch (age > 2*time.Minute) by inducing filesystem latency on /tmp/alive so that ModTime is not updated within the window, causing repeated liveness failures and pod restart loops that each re-submit pending transactions to network nodes, increasing their processing load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that os.Stat('/tmp/ready') is the sole readiness gate, with no cryptographic or integrity check, to cause the readiness probe to oscillate between ready and not-ready states, forcing the load balancer to repeatedly reroute traffic and causing network nodes to handle connection churn exceeding 30% above baseline?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a CrashLoopBackOff by repeatedly causing the liveness probe (os.Stat('/tmp/alive') + age check) to fail, so that each pod restart causes the pinger's submitWithRetry to fire a burst of up to maxRetries+1 transactions at network nodes before the next restart, cumulatively increasing node transaction-processing load by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of argument count validation (only len(os.Args) > 1 is checked) to pass an arbitrarily long string as os.Args[1], causing the default switch branch to execute fmt.Fprintln to stderr with that string, and if the orchestrator logs stderr to a shared logging pipeline, cause log-processing resource exhaustion that indirectly delays heartbeat writes and triggers liveness failures?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary performs no locking around os.Stat('/tmp/alive'), so that concurrent invocations of the healthcheck binary race with the heartbeat goroutine writing /tmp/alive, causing intermittent liveness failures that trigger pod restarts and amplify network node load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 'live' mode reads fi.ModTime() from /tmp/alive without verifying file content integrity, so that replacing /tmp/alive with a zero-byte file having an old timestamp causes the liveness probe to fail, triggering a pod restart that forces re-establishment of all SDK connections to network nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a maximum argument length check on os.Args[1] to supply a multi-megabyte string, causing the fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, 'usage: healthcheck [ready|live]') write to block on a full stderr pipe, hanging the healthcheck process and causing the Kubernetes probe timeout to fire, which is treated as a liveness failure and triggers a pod restart?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that os.Stat('/tmp/alive') returns an error when the file is deleted (not just stale), causing the 'live' branch to print the raw error to stderr and exit 1, and if this is triggered repeatedly via a shared /tmp volume, cause continuous pod restarts that flood network nodes with new connection requests?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary uses time.Since(fi.ModTime()) which depends on the system clock, so that a container clock skew attack (e.g., via a shared NTP misconfiguration) makes the age appear greater than 2*time.Minute even for a freshly written file, causing persistent liveness failures and continuous pod restart loops that amplify network node load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the readiness probe only checks for file existence (os.Stat('/tmp/ready')) with no TTL or freshness check, so that once the file exists the pod is permanently marked ready even if the pinger is in a degraded state, causing all transaction load to remain on a single pod and overloading the network nodes it targets?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 'live' mode's staleness threshold is hardcoded at 2*time.Minute with no jitter, so that if multiple pinger replicas are deployed with synchronized restart times, they all restart simultaneously and each re-establishes connections to the same network nodes, causing a synchronized connection storm exceeding 30% resource increase?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary exits with code 2 for unknown modes, and some orchestrators treat exit code 2 differently from exit code 1 (e.g., as a fatal error rather than a probe failure), causing the pod to be terminated and restarted rather than just marked unhealthy, leading to more aggressive restart behavior and higher network node load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that os.Stat('/tmp/alive') is called without any retry or grace period in the healthcheck binary, so that a transient filesystem hiccup (e.g., tmpfs remount) causes a single liveness probe failure that immediately triggers a pod restart, and if this is repeatable, causes a sustained restart loop that keeps network nodes busy with connection setup?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary writes error details to os.Stderr (fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, err)) including the raw filesystem error message, and if stderr is forwarded to a centralized logging system, cause log injection that triggers alerting workflows which in turn restart pods and increase network node load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 'ready' mode exits 0 as long as /tmp/ready exists regardless of whether the pinger is actually submitting transactions successfully, causing the orchestrator to keep sending traffic to a pod that is silently failing and retrying all transactions (up to maxRetries+1 times each) against network nodes, increasing their load?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the liveness probe checks /tmp/alive freshness but the heartbeat goroutine writes to /tmp/alive every 15 seconds, so that if the pinger process is CPU-starved (e.g., by a noisy neighbor container), the heartbeat goroutine is delayed beyond 2 minutes, causing a liveness failure and pod restart that re-floods network nodes with new transactions?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary has no mechanism to detect or report that the pinger's transaction submission is in a retry storm (submitWithRetry exhausting all maxRetries), so the readiness probe continues to return 0 while the pinger hammers network nodes with repeated failed transactions, increasing their load by over 30%?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 'live' mode uses os.Stat (which follows symlinks) on /tmp/alive, so that if /tmp/alive is replaced with a symlink to a file with an old modification time, the liveness probe fails and triggers a pod restart that causes network nodes to handle a burst of new connection and transaction requests?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary performs no validation that os.Args[0] (the binary path) is the expected binary, so that if the binary is replaced or wrapped in a container image, it can be made to always return exit 0 for liveness while the pinger is actually in a runaway retry loop flooding network nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'live' mode does not check whether the pinger's ticker goroutine is actually running, only that /tmp/alive was recently written, so that a scenario where the ticker is blocked (e.g., waiting on a slow network node response) is invisible to the liveness probe, allowing the pinger to accumulate pending transactions that all fire simultaneously when unblocked?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary's 'ready' mode has no timeout on os.Stat('/tmp/ready'), so that if /tmp is mounted on a slow or hung NFS volume, the readiness probe hangs indefinitely, causing the orchestrator to treat it as a failure and restart the pod, which then re-establishes connections to network nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Increasing network processing node resource consumption by at least 30% without brute force actions, compared to the preceding 24 hours'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary does not validate the number of arguments beyond len(os.Args) > 1, so that passing multiple arguments (e.g., 'live extra args') still selects the 'live' mode via os.Args[1], but the extra arguments could be used to fingerprint or probe the container environment in ways that assist further resource-amplification attacks?
